draft.introduction: Cassiodorus’s Institutions of Divine and Secular Learning (c. 560 CE) is a guide for monks on what to study, integrating biblical scholarship with the liberal arts. It became the blueprint for monastic education and the preservation of classical learning.

translation.text: Perceiving that the schools were swarming with students eager for secular learning, I was deeply grieved that Holy Scripture had no public teachers. I attempted to establish in Rome, with Pope Agapetus, a school for sacred studies. But since this could not be accomplished due to the wars that raged, I have written these books as a substitute.
translation.original_text: Cum studia saecularium litterarum magno desiderio fervere cognoscerem

translator_notes.note: Cassiodorus’s program of combining sacred and secular learning in the monastery effectively saved classical education when the Roman school system collapsed. His scriptorium at Vivarium became a model for monastic copying that preserved ancient texts through the Dark Ages.
